event: Christ rescuing Peter from sinking in the sea
symbols: Christ walking on water — divine power over the chaos of the sea and the forces of nature
Peter sinking — the failure of faith when eyes turn from Christ to circumstances
Christ's outstretched hand grasping Peter — divine rescue of the faltering believer
stormy sea — the trials and tribulations that threaten to overwhelm faith
boat with disciples — the Church tossed by storms yet preserved
dark sky and turbulent waters — the darkness of doubt and fear
description: A dramatic nighttime scene showing Christ standing on turbulent dark waters, reaching out to grasp the hand of Peter who is sinking into the sea. Christ wears a red robe with a blue mantle and stands firmly on the waves. Peter, partially submerged, reaches desperately upward with both hands toward Christ, his orange-red robe billowing in the water. Behind them, a boat full of disciples struggles in the rough sea, the sail billowing. A dark, rocky shoreline with a castle or fortification is visible in the background under a stormy sky. The scene is rendered with dramatic chiaroscuro.
theological_description: The episode of Peter walking on water and sinking (Matthew 14:22-33) is one of the Gospels' most vivid illustrations of the relationship between faith and doubt. After Jesus walked on the sea toward the disciples' storm-tossed boat, Peter boldly asked, 'Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the water.' Jesus said, 'Come.' Peter walked on the water, 'but when he saw the wind, he was afraid, and beginning to sink he cried out, Lord, save me. Jesus immediately reached out his hand and took hold of him, saying, O you of little faith, why did you doubt?' (Matthew 14:29-31). The painting captures the precise moment of rescue — Christ's firm hand grasping Peter's desperate reach. Theologically, this scene teaches that faith fixed on Christ can accomplish the impossible, but when attention shifts to circumstances (the wind and waves), faith falters. Yet even Peter's failure reveals grace: Jesus does not let him drown but immediately rescues him. The scene concludes with the disciples' confession: 'Truly you are the Son of God' (Matthew 14:33). Walking on water demonstrates Christ's sovereignty over chaos — the sea in Hebrew thought symbolized primordial disorder — echoing Psalm 77:19: 'Your way was through the sea, your path through the great waters.'
